title: practical english usage - Part 349
content:
As/who/that can be left out when they refer to the object of the following verb.

He's wearing the same shirt that/as he had on yesterday.

of the following verb.

As is also possible before a clause, especially with a noun that is the object vocabulary •  574  see I'm seeing a lot of things in this book that I missed when I read it before.

I'm seeing much  better since I got those new glasses.

Progressive forms can be used for changes in people’ s ability to see.

I see they're talking about putting up taxes again.

‘ We've got a problem.' ‘ 1 see.' normally used.

When  see means ‘ understand’  or ‘ have heard’  (►  482), progressive forms arc not ‘ Look! A camel!’ ‘ You're seeing things.' imagining things that are not there.

But we  can say that somebody is seeing things if we  mean that he/she is I can see an aeroplane. (AmE  also I see an airplane.) used, especially in British English (►  84).

To  talk about seeing something at the moment of speaking, can see is often not Do you see the woman in blue over there? (     Are-you seeing-...?) not normally used.

When see means ‘ perceive with one’ s eyes’ , progressive (‘ continuous’ ) forms are 1  progressive         forms     not   used 574 see from state schools.

elite group of expensive private schools such as Eton College - very different university-level institutions. Note also that in Britain, the public schools are an name is likely to be a secondary school. In American English, school includes e.g. London School of Economics). A  British institution with High School in its (though university departments may have the word School in their name - In British English, school is not used for a university or similar institution 573 school and  no                   ►   iao scarcely,        hardly             sooner For so after say and tell, ►    5«6.

Has she said who's coming? He only told one person where the money was.

But say and tell can introduce the answers to questions.

to see-a-film.  James-told me-wheiher-...) not James asked whether I wanted to see a film. (     James said-whether I wanted Neither tell nor say can introduce indirect questions (►  260).

5  indirect      questions not I told the children to go away, (     I said-the children to go away.) Say cannot be used like this.

Tell can be used before object + infinitive, in the sense of‘ order’  or ‘ instruct’ .

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary' •  575  see, look (at) and watch Look where you 're going.

Look (at) what you've done!  Look who's here!

Note that at is often dropped before a u/h-clause.

not                                                not Look at me! (     Leek me!) Look! (     Leek at!) preposition. Compare: When look has an object it is followed by at. When  there is no object there is no He looked at her with his eyes full of love.

'Do you see that man? 'Yes.' 'Look again.' 'Oh no! It's Moriarty!' / looked at the photo, but I didn't see anybody I knew.

look at something deliberately. Compare: what is there. You can see something without wanting to, but you can only We   use look to talk about concentrating, paying attention, trying to see 2  look     (at) Did you see the article about the strike in today's paper?

Suddenly I saw something strange, (     Suddenly I looked at not we  are paying attention.

See is the ordinary verb to say that something 'comes to our eyes', whether or 1  see 575  see,    look     (at)  and   watch etc   with        that-dause         ►   ««2 see,    hear, For see + object + infinitive l-ing form, ►     110.

See if you can get him  to stop talking.

See if.. . can often means 'try to'.

You'd better see about that with Jim.

We'll see about that tomorrow, (     We^l-sec that tomorrow?) A  preposition is necessary before an object in these cases.

I'll call the restaurant and see if they've got a table free.

'What time do you want to go to the gym? 'Let me see. How aboutfwe 'Can I use the car tonight? 'I'll see.' See can mean 'consider', 'think', 'find out', 'discuss' or 'decide'.

4 other     meanings:      'consider',     etc Jack's down at the docks. He's seeing that our stuff gets loaded correctly.
